data.title: Broadcom: My Best AI Investment Idea For 2026
data.description:
As the AI revolution pivots from raw GPU power toward specialized connectivity and custom silicon, Broadcom (AVGO) is emerging as a critical infrastructure linchpin.

Much like the rapid ascent of specialized neoclouds, like Nebius (NBIS) or CoreWeave (CRWV), Broadcom is entering a high-growth phase that could see its valuation scale significantly as it captures the lion's share of the AI networking and data center markets.

While the long-term outlook for Broadcom looks great, the investment thesis got a bit of shade last week after the business revealed expectations of lower gross margins short-term. This revelation sparked a selloff for Broadcom, which sets up a buying opportunity.

Broadcom Seeing A Massive Increase In Net Revenue Due To Demand For XPUs

Broadcom’s AI revenues are surging as the business benefits from hyper-scalers doubling down on their investment spending. In Q4 2025 Broadcom posted total net revenue of $18.0 billion, up 28% YoY.

While net revenue growth was impressive all on its own, Broadcom is enjoying a significant profit explosion as well: its net income soared 97% YoY, while its EPS growth was an equally-impressive 93% YoY.

A growth lever for Broadcom is the increasing reliance of data centers on XPUs, which are AI accelerators. These accelerators are used to run large-language models as well as other AI workloads in the cloud. The growth in Broadcom’s net revenue is partially attributable to the sale of custom XPUs, which are used in data centers to offload specialized AI tasks from general-purpose CPUs and GPUs, significantly improving energy efficiency and lowering the cost-per-token for inference.

XPUs are Application-Specific Integrated Circuits and fulfill a different role in data centers than GPUs; they are specifically engineered to optimize inference workloads and high-frequency data processing, allowing hyperscalers to run massive AI models at a fraction of the operating cost required by general-purpose hardware.

Broadcom manufactures XPUs in collaboration with hyperscaler companies like Meta Platforms or Alphabet, which are adopting XPUs for both training large language models and monetizing via inference.

Alphabet, for example, works with Broadcom on the v7 "Ironwood" chip, which is going to be a major revenue driver for the hardware company in the future. Broadcom co-develops the core TPU architecture that Google deploys with CGP (Google Cloud Platform), which positions the business for long-term growth in its core market.

All of this happens in the context of an unprecedented investment craze in the data center market. Data centers are blossoming like mushrooms after a heavy rainy season. The spending forecast issued by the IDC highlights this explosion of pent-up demand in the data center market.

According to IDC, AI infrastructure spending is set to grow to more than $200 billion annually within the next three years. The market expansion in servers will support demand for custom AI accelerators, a particular strength of Broadcom.

Margins Are Likely Not A Major Issue

Broadcom did cause a ruckus with investors a week ago when its forward margin guidance pointed to modest near-term profitability pressure

The main point of contention last week was that Broadcom’s management guided for a sequential 100 basis point (1%) decline in its gross margins in Q1 2026 which is triggered by a shift in the business’s revenue composition. Broadcom is generating a higher share of AI revenue, which carries lower gross margins.

As custom AI silicon and early-stage accelerator ramps carry lower gross margins than the company’s core networking and software segments, Broadcom is subjected to near-term margin pressure.

As the business gets ready to ramp up its shipments of (lower-margin) custom AI accelerators to hyper-scale customers in 2026, these shipments are expected to have a negative short-term margin impact. Considering how small the projected margin drop is, I have come to the conclusion that the market exaggerated the margin impact for Q1 2026.

Fair Value Estimate And My Game Plan For AVGO

Broadcom has a strong market position in interconnects and custom ASIC infrastructure which makes the business a promising investment choice for you if you believe that XPUs/customer AI accelerators are going to see significant forward demand.

I am leaning towards a strong buy rating for Broadcom, for obvious reasons: the business is in a great setup, with demand for XPUs poised to grow for many years. Furthermore, Broadcom is earnings-strong: in Q4 2025 the business made $8.5 billion in net income alone.

Broadcom has a FWD Price-To-Earnings Ratio of 24.5 with the recent selloff in the market after Q4 2025 earnings having a cooling effect on the company’s previously very high P/E ratio.

Broadcom operates on a fiscal year that does not align with the standard calendar year. The next ‘next year’ classification used in the text as well as in the table below relates to the 2027 fiscal year, which starts on November 2, 2026. I am using this forward-looking EPS-based valuation approach in order to smooth out earnings outliers in the short term.

NVIDIA has a FWD Price-To-Earnings Ratio of 24.2 and AMD (AMD), whose data center business has started to boom in 2025, has a FWD Price-To-Earnings Ratio of 33.0.

In my opinion, Broadcom’s correction is a chance to scoop up shares, especially because the guided-for margin drop is relatively minor and does not in any way, shape or form derail Broadcom’s widely profitable hardware businesses.

I opened a small position in Broadcom at $335 a share, which is equal to a 24 FWD P/E. My initial position accounts for just 1% of my investable assets, so Broadcom’s performance is going to have only a very minor impact on my growth portfolio. My exposure, therefore, is very much limited, and I have cash on reserve in the event that Broadcom’s shares extend their correction in the coming weeks.

My game plan for Broadcom is to keep it simple and to add on dips. I plan on ramping up my purchases of Broadcom if the Relative Strength Index drops below 30, a threshold level indicating oversold technical sentiment in the market.

In my opinion, Broadcom could be worth 25-30 FWD earnings (= Fair Value Estimate of $347 and $416 a share), recognizing the strong upward trend in AI semiconductor revenue, strength of hyper-scaler partnerships (Meta and Google), and high profitability.

I don’t view the margin guidance as especially off-putting either and think that investors probably overreacted here last week. If Broadcom can grow its net revenue, led by AI semiconductor revenues, at double digits for a few more years, the ASIC/XPU specialist could be worth much more than that.

Risk Analysis

Capex spending and a moderating pace of data center build-outs are downside risks for you if you are thinking about opening up a position in Broadcom. So far, capex spending is not fragile, but this may change in the future.

While I made it a core element of my strong buy thesis that the margin compression is not a reason to panic, a successive margin drop would likely be viewed very negatively.

My Verdict: Strong Buy

Broadcom represents an AI-driven, high-upside play for you if you believe in the potential of custom AI accelerators in the data center market, which is where capex spending is blossoming.

Broadcom enters 2026 with a staggering $73 billion AI backlog, which is tied to multi-billion-dollar investment partnerships with major hyperscale customers, including Google and Anthropic, and its aggressive efforts in helping them develop high-performing XPUs.

Broadcom’s strong position with respect to XPUs, which are critically important hardware components in the server stack, makes shares a strong buy for me. The margin compression does not seem to be a major issue in the grand scheme of things, in my opinion, and I am ready to buy the dips.

data.title: HP Inc.: Severely Mispriced Cash Flow Powerhouse
data.description:
Introduction

Last time I covered HP Inc. (HPQ), I highlighted their strong fundamentals, backed by excellent cash flows despite significant tariff uncertainty and rising macroeconomic challenges, with the stock trading at a significant discount to fair value.

With the stock down ~15% since I last covered them to almost exactly the same level they fell to back when tariffs were announced, accelerated cost savings, very strong cash flows despite significant macro headwinds, a dividend hike, and still very solid value, I’m upgrading HPQ to a Strong Buy, with a buying order placed recently.

Internal Developments

As expected, HPQ delivered a solid Q4 report, with $2.9 billion in free cash flow (-2%) on the same net cash provided by operating activities as in FY24 ($3.7 billion; i.e. higher CAPEX) despite the very uncertain environment affected by tariffs that prompted the company to adapt significantly, as mentioned below.

This comes as the company now highlighted $1 billion in AI-driven gross run-rate cost savings over the next 3 years, with 4,000 to 6,000 job cuts expected following the ~9,400 cuts seen from November 2022 to October 2025. This is on top of HP surpassing their previous cost saving target of $1.4 billion in FY25 and delivering $2.2 billion in cumulative gross annualized savings.

As for FY26, we continue to see very solid expectations, with a similar $2.8 billion to $3 billion in free cash flow expected this year as well, with revenue expected to grow faster than the market and EPS affected by higher memory costs.

Thanks to this strong “odds-defying” performance including AI PC growing to ~30% of their PC mix, and in spite of the issues in printers, HP increased their dividend by ~4%, to $0.30 per quarter, getting the current yield to a very attractive ~5.2%. This comes as HP returned ~66% of their free cash flow (i.e., $1.9 billion) in dividends and buybacks in FY25, for a combined yield of ~8.9% today - which is excellent for such a small payout ratio, even though it was much smaller than the ~$3.175 billion returned in FY24.

Financially, based on HPQ’s latest report, we see an overall weak position at first glance, with current assets below the current liabilities and ~$8.82 billion in long-term debt. This leaves the equity in the negative $346 million, although we should take into account that the current brand value isn’t included there and we’re talking about a very asset-light business model. Again, it’s normal for them to not have that many assets, such as production plants and property, which has ultimately proven to be an advantage in this environment that allowed them to quickly adapt their logistics to the tariff headwinds while also holding a massive $3.7 billion in cash.

Macro Developments

As mentioned in the previous articles, rate cuts should be a significant catalyst for HPQ, with three cuts seen in 2025 and hopeful expectations for more in 2026. Again, given the company’s relatively high level of debt that’s generating ~$430 million worth of interest expenses TTM, the lower the rates, the better, as the company will have to refinance it over time, with each delay literally costing them money.

Meanwhile, as the company depends on businesses and consumers, rate cuts (globally) could also lead to operational improvements coming from more demand, as discretionary budgets would also rise for these clients. We’re talking about several years of stagnant or even decreasing revenues and profits for virtually every company that depends on discretionary budgets, especially in the US and China, with HP not being a stranger to this alongside companies like Nike (NKE), LVMH (LVMHF), etc.

However, keep in mind that rate cuts alone aren’t enough to push debt costs lower. There are several other factors that can affect the borrowing costs of countries and, subsequently, companies, although HP has the advantage of being able to tap lower-cost debt from Europe through euro bonds.

Regarding the US, we still saw some uncertainty persist in the market, which was recently pricing some uncertainty around the new Fed Chair (Powell’s term ends in May 2026) and worrying about their independence. Again, even though this person has 1 vote out of 12 in FOMC rate decisions, Powell has proven just how much attention the market is paying to this person’s words, at times causing more volatility than even the President of the United States.

If the new Chair is just obediently saying what Trump wants during such an environment, the market’s opinion could be affected even more, potentially leading to higher perceived risks - alongside other geopolitical tensions.

Tariffs have also been a major issue for HPQ in 2025, forcing the company to respond quickly and move their manufacturing capacity outside of China to bypass tariffs. Despite the stock initially falling about 13% in a single day back in April, when the tariffs were announced in expectation of their effect, HP was able to withstand these quite well, showing excellent decision-making abilities under pressure, basically remodeling its entire logistic chain and focusing on new manufacturing hubs like Thailand (now a primary hub for consumer PCs), Vietnam, or Mexico.

Tariffs are very likely not the end goal, of course, and we’re probably looking at more of a short- or medium-term detractor that will also bring back US production, but seeing the company react so quickly to this definitely says a lot about their management. Not only that, but with the stock now trading at basically the exact same level as when tariffs were initially announced, it makes the stock even more attractive, while the business continues to deliver massive cash flows.

But, even without accounting for potential supply chain issues and higher costs from tariffs, we’re still looking at rising issues in the market, with spikes in prices for memory chips and solid-state drivers, which prompted HP and Dell (DELL) to increase prices recently. AI demand has significant effects on this, causing major component manufacturers [like Samsung or Micron (MU)] to divert their limited factory capacity away from standard consumer-related memory and toward high-margin AI server chips, basically leaving companies like HP in a position where they have to fight over a shrinking supply of standard components.

Still, we are far from an environment where people and businesses would easily afford replacing PCs even without this issue, especially after the pandemic and stay-at-home period basically accelerated the previous replacement cycle. In HP’s case, we’re also talking about an overall high level of quality on top that would make it even less likely for someone to purchase a new laptop after buying the previous one in 2021, for example.

This is definitely something to pay close attention to, as it will ultimately have effects on the company’s clients, potentially coming at a bad time that could cause even more delays. We see some signs of this cycle improving given the massive growth seen especially in AI PCs, and it’s after all an inevitable thing, which is why I remain optimistic in this case despite the potential delays caused by the current environment.

This has been a reality for most companies that depended on consumer budgets, although we’ve seen some optimistic results when the US GDP grew by ~4.3% in Q3 based on recent data, well above expectations thanks especially to strong consumer spending. The market didn’t really react to the data since it’s backward-looking (July-September), on top of worries about what’s actually driving this increase.

There are comments from economists pointing out the “K-shaped” economic growth behind it, as higher-income households are the ones driving growth while middle- and lower-class ones struggle, while inflation re-emerged in the months after this data. As a note, just keep in mind that GDP growth doesn’t mean the population is doing better, but rather measures the total volume of economic activity.

Risks Reiterated

To reiterate, keep in mind that even though HP was able to withstand the current tariff headwinds to a large degree and was quick to adapt, it doesn’t mean they won’t be caught in a position where these won’t affect them in the future, as it remains a rapidly developing and uncertain environment, especially with the significant headwind from rising memory costs.

This can put pressure on the company’s margins, potentially hurting them beyond their cost saving targets if we see macro issues spreading to their clients in an even weaker environment.

Valuation

Starting with the midpoint of $2.9 billion expected in FCF in FY26, I’ll assume a 4.5% CAGR over the first 5 years thanks to cost savings and improvements in the environment that could come from the ultimately inevitable renewal cycle and AI advancements, followed by a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years for similar reasons, with a 2.25% terminal growth rate.

I’ll go again for a WACC of 9% (assumptions: ~10.7% cost of equity, ~4% cost of debt), with $5.96 billion in net debt and 953 million shares outstanding as of the Q4 report.

This way, we get to an enterprise value of $50.35 billion, an equity value of $44.39 billion, and an intrinsic value of $46.58 per share, which is roughly double the current $22.93 at the moment of writing this, leaving a significant margin of safety even with much lower growth assumptions.

Conclusions

HP has proven to be a well-run business that’s able to adapt to a very uncertain environment while still delivering massive cash flows and a solid performance, while strong shareholder returns continue. There are risks, especially from the uncertainty around component costs, but the price seems to already price in significant discounts, making the risk-reward very attractive in my opinion.

data.title: Entergy Corporation: A Regulated Growth Utility, Not An Income Stock
data.description:
Why Entergy’s Investment Case Has Changed

Entergy Corporation (ETR) has generally been seen as a steady but slightly boring regulated utility. Earlier, its stability was what attracted investors - not growth. That is now changing. Over the past few years - as seen in its latest financials - Entergy is pivoting more towards growth.

Earlier, Entergy’s earnings graph moved up or down with weather issues, the occasional rate case, and mostly storm-related capital recovery. Whatever growth there was, was obscured by this kind of volatility. Today, that narrative is increasingly changing towards structural load growth driven by large industrial customers and data center demand. In the most recent quarter, Entergy’s weather-adjusted retail sales grew at 4.5%, with industrial sales growing by more than 7%. Unlike weather-related business, load growth has a compounding quality. That drives rate base expansion and, ultimately, a properly balanced demand-infrastructure synergy drives earnings growth.

This current growth phase is distinctive by how the growth is now quantifiable. It is also visible in forward planning. Management informs us that Entergy’s data-center pipeline is now between 7-12 gigawatts. The expansion is fueled by active leads that should translate into service agreements over the next couple of years. Importantly, these forecasts exclude large hyperscalers, so what’s reported is rather conservative.

This is having an effect on capital deployment as well. The company currently has capital plans for $41 billion through 2029. Much of these funds are growth-focused, related to customer demand, and not merely maintenance or repair. The company has already secured over 19 gigawatts of generation equipment - capacity well beyond current forecasts. Strategy is to use funds generated from customers to serve their demands, which is a distinctive trait that separates Entergy from peers.

How Regulated Utilities Create Growth - and Where Entergy Fits

Before we go into Entergy’s evolving demand profile, let me quickly talk about how growth happens in something as stable and unexciting as a regulated electric utility. Regulated utilities cannot expand margin or raise price as they see fit, unlike unregulated ones. Here, the key ingredient of earnings growth is rate base expansion, which is the total value of utility assets on which regulators allow returns.

Three aspects - capital, regulator allowed returns, and cost recovery timeline - determine earnings growth. When they all come together, earnings grows strongly. When they don’t, even an operationally solid company could stagnate.

Load growth sits even upstream of this process, fueling capital investment. As demand goes up, capital is invested in generation, transmission, and distribution, and the whole thing is added to the rate base. This makes residential demand less important than long-term and contracted and capital-intensive industrial and data-center demand. Here, utilities deploy larger amounts of capital per unit of demand, so their rate base typically increases.

We see this distinction in Entergy’s recent operating data. We note that industrial electricity sales increased by over 7% year over year, much more than the retail growth of around 4.5% on a weather-adjusted basis. That mix shift is important because it supports sustained rate-base expansion rather than weather-based consumption volatility.

Even in mature markets, utilities can deliver high single digit earnings growth if they can combine above-average load growth with predictable recovery. We will evaluate Entergy’s business across this framework.

Geography and Regulation: Why Entergy’s Footprint Matters

Entergy operates in Arkansas, Mississippi, Louisiana (including New Orleans), and Texas - each jurisdiction is different, with different load growths, different regulatory settings, and capital recovery. There’s one positive aspect in common across all four states - none of them have heavily constrained regulatory enviroments like in the coasts.

Mississippi has become one of Entergy’s strongest growth geographies. There is strong industrial demand, and a state-level regulatory framework of expedited approval for large projects. This has funded new infrastructure while lowering overall customer rates. For example, the company estimated that rates were 16% lower than they would have been otherwise, only because new industrial customers absorbed the additional capital costs.

Arkansas also has a stable regulatory structure with meaningful industrial/technology demand. The state has new legislation (the Generating Arkansas Jobs Act) that accelerates approval of projects that generate new jobs. This reduces regulatory lag between investment and recovery. The company also benefits from existing baseload assets, which lower the marginal cost of serving new load. That also supports rate-base growth. It sounds contradictory - this apparent tension between lowering marginal costs and growing rate base by capital investment - but it is not, really. Regulators support capital efficiency, not capital maximization. They will penalize utilities with more capital per MW. So good utility businesses aim for the most regulatory-approved capital per certain MW.

Louisiana, including Entergy New Orleans, is politically more complex but also significant economically. Louisiana has large industrial customers linked to LNG, Chemicals and data infra. Louisiana has more regulatory scrutiny. That may cause some delays and rate limits. However, Entergy manages to find customer-funded projects, which eases scrutiny from regulators.

Texas is by far Entergy’s fastest growing territory by population and peak demand. Entergy Texas just recently hit new peak loads, so they are building more capacity to support that growth. Regulators are aligned with distribution cost recovery for utility companies, so companies realize earnings as they spend capital.

Its particular geographies of operation give Entergy a unique benefit - growth-friendly regulators and high demand markets at the same time. This explains how its earnings outlook is improving.

Translating Demand Into Earnings and Cash Flow

This improved outlook is beginning to meaningfully translate to measurable earnings and cash-flow. It is not just a forward-looking narrative any longer. The company reported adjusted EPS of $1.53 in its most recent quarter, beating expectations. Management raised the lower bound of full-year earnings guidance by $0.10. This suggests confidence in earnings durability, given this was the seasonally strongest quarter which prompted the raised guidance.

Entergy also generated $3.81 billion in revenue at the operating level, which is around 12% yoy growth. This was primarily driven by higher sales volumes and customer-funded investments. Now, in a regulated model, operating leverage requires that incremental revenues beat cost growth. This was exactly what we see in this quarter at Entergy, with the gains we saw achieved despite higher operating and maintenance costs.

Entergy also saw meaningful improvements in cash generation. In the entire year, the company monetized over $535 million of nuclear production tax credits. This provided a non-dilutive source of ready liquidity.

The company also managed equity issuance proactively. They issue equity forward contracts which informs investors early on when dilution is going to happen. This is different from unplanned open market dilution. This helps the company lock in a specific amount of dilution, for example, the $800 million they settled in Q3, and an additional $330 million they settled at quarter-end. This methodical planning helps investors get more visibility on future dilutions. Risk is not risk, simply business necessity, if it is pre-planned and quantified.

Peer Comparison

Utility peer comparison should not be done simply because two companies share an industry label. In Entergy’s case, we need to filter peers by regulatory structure, earnings profile and capital recovery, not just industry or market cap. The peer set visible on Seeking Alpha:

Entergy is grouped with Xcel Energy (XEL), Exelon Corporation (EXC), PG&E Corporation (PCG), NRG Energy (NRG), and SSE plc (SSEZY). This list is useful and mechanistically correct, but an analysis of which subset is a real peer helps understand Entergy better.

Simply by scale, each of these are peers, with Entergy sitting in the top tier along with Xcel and Exelon, in the low-to-mid $40 billion range, well above most regional players. This immediately tells us that Entergy is no pushover, no niche operator. The EV comparison is more interesting - and divergent. Exelon’s higher EV reflects its wires-heavy structure, which means higher capital spends. Entergy and Xcel are closer, illustrating their vertically integrated nature. Both companies monetize generation and load growth directly, in contrast to Exelon post separation.

After its separation from Constellation Energy, Exelon became a mainly wires-only utility. It no longer generates energy, it simply owns transmission and distribution. So Exelon only monetizes load growth through higher grid utilization, and does not monetize generation. Entergy, on the other hand, invests in owned generation as demand rises, plus transmission and distribution, so this entire vertically integrated structure adds to rate base. This is a key structural difference which allows Entergy (and Xcel) to deploy more capital per unit of load and grow its earnings more directly.

At the business model level, as well, Xcel comes closest to Entergy. Both are regulated utilities, both rely on rate-base expansion for earnings growth, and both operate in jurisdictions where regulators are more aligned with capital recovery in reasonable time. However, there’s a subtle but critical difference in the sources of growth. Xcel’s growth comes from policy-led renewable and transmission spending. Entergy’s comes from new industrial and data-center customers with long-term contracts. So Entergy’s growth is tied to paying customers, not policy mandates. This helps because paying customers under long term contracts reduce risk and improve earnings visibility. Customer-funded projects also help reduce regulatory pushbacks while improving the balance sheet.

PG&E works with a different model, focusing more on wildfire liabilities and regulatory scrutiny. NRG Energy is different too; it is mostly a merchant and retail power company, driven by power prices and not by regulated rate-base growth. SSE makes an interesting contrast, operating in the UK under a very different political and regulatory regimen.

Two other utilities not on this list but more directly comparable to Entergy are Dominion Energy (D) and Southern Company (SO). These are all large regulated utility companies. They all offer everything from generation, transmission to distribution. They only invest capital when there is a specific demand. There is, however, a key difference. Dominion’s growth is tied to population trends in the East Coast only. Southern focuses more on large generation projects with one-off demands. Entergy’s growth phase is cleaner. It grows through incremental industrial and data-center demands.

Put together, Entergy stands out for a more visible growth phase, customer-backed funding for new projects, and overall a friendlier regulatory setting allowing for quicker recovery.

In terms of valuation, trading as it does at 22-23x trailing earnings, Entergy does not look cheap compared to the high-teens of the broad utility sector. However, this does not capture the underlying change we discussed today. Entergy is growing differently from a median regulated utility. Its forward growth-adjusted valuation looks more reasonable in this context, with a non-GAAP fwd PEG of below 2.1, which is below the sector median and, more importantly, the company’s own historical average. PEG is not a conventional valuation metric for slow-growth utilities, but given ETR’s guided 8+% growth, PEG is the exactly correct metric here.

Key Risks to the Growth and Valuation Thesis

Entergy’s key risk is execution, not demand. Any delays in permitting or construction etc will hurt their capital expansion plan. A second risk is also related to delays, but in regulatory ones. While Entergy operates in constructive jurisdictions, cost recovery is not instantaneous. If there’s too much lag between capital deployment and recovery, we might see pressure on near-term cash flow.

A third risk is customer concentration. A critical portion of Entergy’s growth is tied to large industrial and data center customers. Though these sign long-term agreements, delays or cancellations across these growth sectors could cause much damage to the company’s growth trajectory.

Finally, valuation itself is a risk. As we noted, the stock is trading above sector multiples, so Entergy has less room for errors than peers. Plus, such relative valuation hurts sentiment, which may be detrimental for investors looking to exit.

Conclusion: A Different Kind of Regulated Utility

Entergy is no longer a slow-growing, weather-sensitive utility. Its earnings profile is increasingly shaped by large scale, long term load growth, customer-funded deployment, and regulatory support for timely recovery. So we see stronger earnings, improved guidance and a viable path to greater than 8% long-term earnings growth.

This, more than anything else, explains its current valuation premium. Entergy is being priced as a regulated entity with visible growth, yet the premium only implies this is a growth it has currently not yet achieved.

So, investors wanting to capture that growth, and not focused on the small ~2.78% yield, will do well to consider Entergy. Entergy represents a differentiated, and vertically integrated, way to access regulated infrastructure growth.

data.title: Transformative AI Makes Sanmina A Strong Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Sanmina Corporation (NASDAQ: SANM) is evolving from a low margin electronics manufacture into a stealth winner in the AI hardware boom. And let me start by stating that in regard to SANM, my thesis is simple. Sanmina's growing exposure to AI centric manufacturing, highlighted by its transformative acquisition of ZT systems will turbocharge growth and earnings. Yet, when I look at the valuation, the stock price does not fully reflect this opportunity. And that's why I am giving SANM a Strong Buy rating.

The company's core business is steady and profitable and new significant catalysts, which I will speak about later, provide a substantial upside. As of now, I believe the market is valuing SANM like a sleepy contract manufacturer and not an emerging leader in cloud and AI infrastructure. This is why I see an opportunity due to the disconnect. At the end of the day, Sanmina is no longer just making circuit boards in the shadows. It is now building the backbone of the AI revolution. I will detail why SANM's AI manufacturing pivot and solid fundamentals make this company warrant a Strong Buy rating.

Business Overview

Sanmina is a leading electronics manufacturing services [EMS] provider. It is essentially a contract manufacturer for OEMs across diverse industries. About 80% of its $8Bn pre-acquisition revenue came from integrated manufacturing solutions such as printed circuit boards, enclosures and full product assembly. SANM serves a broad set of end markets such as industrial, energy, medical devices, defense, automotive, communication networks and cloud infrastructure. And this diversification means that SANM is not reliant on any single sector or customer. In Q4 2025, the company's top 10 customers accounted for 51.7% of revenue with now single customer with over 10%. Which is a good thing because it significantly reduces customer concentration risk.

Historically, Sanmina was known for slow growth and modest margins. Which is a typical EMS profile. For instance, in 2024 revenues only inched up from $6.6Bn to $7.6Bn. Yet operating margins were low single digits. SANM focused on efficiency and buybacks rather than boosting growth. However, after a 15% sales drop in 2024 amid inventory destocking, SANM entered 2025 with leaner operations and increased demand for their products. And the stage was set for a pivot. Which management delivered successfully.

Today, Sanmina is firing on all its cylinders. Its manufacturing footprint spans the globe including advanced facilities in US [Texas] and Europe [Netherlands]. These additions bring state of the art capacity which has been geared to cloud data centers and AI hardware. As a result, Sanmina has moved up the value chain from making boards to assembling full server and rack systems for hyperscale customers. I would say that the company's vertically integrated model and strong customer service remain key strengths. And now it has scale and the tech know-how of how to tackle the most complex critical hardware programs.

Growth Drivers

So why am I rating Sanmina a Strong Buy? Because SANM's game changer is its expanding role in AI and cloud infrastructure manufacturing. This is majorly though ZT system acquisition. In October 2025, SANM completed the purchase of ZT systems' data center manufacturing business from AMD (AMD).

So why is this acquisition so important? Well, this deal instantly gives Sanmina a much larger presence in the fast growing hyperscale market, including equipment for AI data center. In fact, it is quite hard to overstate how transformative this is as highlighted by the CEO, Jure Sola;

...This is an exciting time for Sanmina as the acquisition of ZT Systems is truly transformative. It increases our scale, expands our capabilities and enables us to capitalize on the significant growth opportunities in the cloud and AI end market...

And this clearly means that Sanmina has just vaulted onto the main stage of AI hardware.

So, why is ZT systems so critical and a game changer for SANM?

ZT systems is a leading manufacturer of custom server and rack systems for cloud giants. And this is exactly the kind of high value business that Sanmina lacked in the past. The unit brings about $5 to $6Bn in annual revenue run ware. And clearly, this effectively doubles SANM size. In fact, management has previously targeted doubling revenue in 3 years but now see an opportunity to do it sooner within 2 years. It is important I note that not only is the quantity of revenue higher, but also the quality of revenue is higher. ZT's operations include large scale integration, advanced liquid cooling tech and close relationships with hyperscalers. And as a result, Sanmina is now assembling full AI server racks and cluster and not just circuit boards.

The second growth driver is the strategic partnership that Sanmina has entered with AMD (AMD) as part of the acquisition deal. AMD chose SANM as its preferred manufacturing partner for new product introduction of cloud and AI solutions. And, honestly, this is a big vote of confidence. AMD effectively handed SANM the keys to build out AI data center hardware for its customer.

And why does it matter? It is because, considering that AMD just announced a multi year deal with OpenAI (OPENAI). This deal was supply 6GW of AI GPUs beginning in 2026. Those GPUs will need to be integrated into racks and clusters for cloud deployment. And SANM is positioned to be the manufacturing arm for such next gen AI systems.

Thirdly, beyond AMD and OpenAI, ZT systems brings other hyperscalers customers. Some of these customers are widely rumored to include the likes of AWS (AMZN) or Meta (META). The CEO highlighted that through ZT, they can partner closely with both hyperscalers and OEM customers across all platforms and technologies. The deal also made AMD a stakeholder in Sanmina. Obviously, this aligns interests for the partnership's success. Sanmina essentially becomes the go to manufacturer for any cloud player looking to roll out AI infrastructure at scale with AMD hardware. And that is a massive new market relative to SANM's historical realm.

It is also important to note that Sanmina closed the ZT systems acquisition ahead of schedule and at a lower net price than initially expected. The final price was $2.05Bn. And Sanmina financed it with attractive debt [by securing a $2.8Bn term loan at reasonable rates] while still keeping net leverage in check. Obviously, this prudent financing means the deal should be immediately accretive to earnings.

Just to put the deal into perspective, I will look into Celestica (CLS) which is a peer EMS company that also pivoted into AI and data center manufacturing. CLS's stock is up over 200% in 2025 and now trades at nearly 46x FWD earnings because investors are pricing explosive growth from AI server demand. Now, when I compare with Sanmina, it is arguably in an even stronger position now. It has a larger revenue scale, a direct partnership with a top GPU maker (AMD) and similar of not greater AI exposure. Yet, as I will show you next, Sanmina's valuation remains far more reasonable.

Valuation

As I mentioned earlier, despite the stock's strong rally this year, Sanmina's valuation is still undemanding relative to its peers and growth prospects. To simplify, I have chosen some of its immediate peers for comparison purposed.

Company TTM P/E FWD P/E FWD EV/EBITDA
Sector Median 31x 31x 15x
Sanmina (SANM) 34X 22X 7X
Plexus (PLXS) 24x 23x 11x
Benchmark (BHE) 43x 47x 11x
Celestica (CLS) 49X 46X 34X

So based on the above table, a few things stand out from the comparison.

First, Sanmina's FWD P/E of 22x is lower than Celestica's 46x and even below smaller peer Plexus at 23x. This is despite SANM likely having a higher FWD growth rate. And to me, it means that the market is pricing SANM like a no growth value stock, which is out of sync with its 46% y/y EPS jump expected in the coming quarter. In fact, Sanmina's FWD Non-GAAP PEG ratio is well under 1.0x. [Celestica TTM GAAP PEG stands at 0.48x while FWD Non-GAAP PEG is at 1.33x]. And this is due to its anticipated growth while most of the other peers don't have meaningful PEG data because their growth is modest or uncertain.

In terms of EV/EBITDA, Sanmina is also lower than most of the other peers. If I compare the TTM EV/EBITDA and FWD EV/EBITDA, there is quite a huge disparity. And this is because of the ZT systems.

If I assume that Sanmina can grow its EPS by 20% CAGR over the next 3-5 years, then a PEG of 1x would equate to a 20x P/E. On $10 FWD earnings, that would translate to $200. Even a PEG of 0.8x would 16x P/E or $160. And this is basically where the stock trades now, despite likely higher growth.

Sanmina's Key Risks

The ZT system acquisition is large and transformational. Combining operations, cultures and supply chains poses challenges, therefore management must smoothly integrate ZT's 2000 employees and multiple facilities. Thus far, the company have kept ZT's veteran leadership which I think is quite encouraging. Nevertheless, a hiccup in integration or unexpected costs could hurt margins. The company has forecasted a smooth integration and kept ZT's management to mitigate this risk.

Second, the other big risk that I see for SANM is AI demand uncertainty. SANM's new growth engine is tied to AI and cloud capex cycles which can be quite volatile. If the current AI investment surge by cloud companies were to slow, SANM lofty growth targets could be pushed out. Specifically, the OpenAI<>AMD deal that partly sparked SANM's rally. If OPENAI's demand or funding decreases, it means that some expected downstream revenue for SANM might be delayed or lost.

Final Thoughts

By aggressively expanding inro AI and cloud infrastructure, Sanmina's story is one of a solid manufacturing business that has suddenly found itself in the right place at the right time. The company is positioned to nearly double its revenue and significantly boost earnings in the next two years. I see Sanmina as a high-quality business with tremendous catalyst in hand, selling at discount. And for these reasons, I give Sanmina Corporation a Strong Buy rating.

data.title: Virtus Newfleet Senior Floating Rate Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

Two developments shaped the loan market in 3Q: a widely expected rate cut in September and retail fund outflows. Despite this, loan yields are still higher than historical average, and steady collateralized loan obligation (CLO) issuance and low net supply kept technicals positive.

Primary syndicated loan activity surged to a record high in 3Q, but 82% consisted of refinancings, repricings, and extensions, resulting in limited net new supply. The repricings were driven by issuers looking to opportunistically shave off borrowing costs; by mid-September, nearly half of performing loans were priced at par or above. However, the sudden collapse of First Brands Group in September had a significant impact on prices, causing the weighted average bid to slip down to 97.06 — down by 13 basis points (bps) from August. Excluding First Brands, the average improves to 97.25—a 6-bps gain month-over-month.

CLO issuance remained active, making 3Q the third-busiest quarter on record. However, issuance slowed toward quarter-end due to limited new supply and tighter loan spreads relative to CLO liability spreads, which weakened the equity arbitrage.

Even accounting for this and retail outflows, demand still outpaced supply by the widest gap in a year.

In terms of fundamentals, overall credit profiles remain disciplined. Average debt/EBITDA held steady at 4.66x, while interest coverage improved to 3.46x overall and 2.81x for leveraged buyouts, both the strongest since 2022. Notably, First Brands was the ninth-largest default in the past five years (including distressed exchanges).

How The Fund Performed

The Fund (Class I) returned 1.22% for the quarter versus the S&P UBS Leveraged Loan Index return of 1.68%.

Contributors

  • Issue selection within aerospace, cable/wireless video, housing, retail, telecommunications, and Split BB credits contributed to performance, as did an overweight in broadcasting.
  • Cash holdings helped performance.

Detractors

  • Weak issue selection within forest products/containers, food/tobacco, gaming/leisure, broadcasting, and consumer non-durables detracted from performance. An underweight to housing was negative.
  • Issue selection within CCC/Split CCC, BB, and Split B credits detracted, as did an overweight to Split BB credits.

Current Fund Strategy

We have gone from being fully invested to raising cash in anticipation of further retail fund outflows and due to the slowing economy. Looking ahead, we will be closely monitoring the government shutdown’s effect on the markets, third quarter earnings season, and economic data releases.
