data.title: Northrop Grumman: An Argument For Buying At Undervaluation (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Way back in 2021-2022, when the defense industry was still undervalued, I bought significant stakes in US defense majors as well as some European ones. I'm talking about companies such as Lockheed (LMT), General Dynamics (GD), Huntington Ingalls (HII), but also Northrop Grumman (NOC).

I've since sold all of these stakes at very good returns - NOC as early as 2023. But when I did, I didn't provide an update on Northrop, which is what I am going to be doing here.

The reason I'm out of these companies (with the exception of a few EU-based defense businesses) is the valuation of the businesses alongside their business models - generally politically-exposed businesses.

As you can see, even selling in 2023, my returns for companies like NOC were absolutely solid. Even if you held for this long alongside my rating, you'd still have beaten the market handily. It's a very clear argument for why the most important quality, next to fundamental safety, is actually the valuation of your investment - i.e., the price you pay.

It's something I unfortunately see overlooked or deprioritized far too often. If you go back to my early article, you can see that by the time I updated, the company was in a negative long-term trend (which made it appealingly valued), and the sentiment at the time of purchase was not that great. Incidentally, this was not long before the Russian invasion of Ukraine. But even without this, I argue the company would have seen very impressive return trends over time, based on that valuation.

It's what makes exiting the investment equally important based on valuation. You will note that, based on the time of my exit, the return from 2023-2025 has actually been quite poor.

By reinvesting that capital into better return potential, I not only avoided the downturn but also generated even higher returns than you see above, or in my last article, which you can find here.

So, let's look at what NOC gives us here.

Northrop Grumman - Can the future provide the growth that the valuation requires?

By my rating in the title, you already know that I'm moving to a "HOLD" on this company as of year-end 2025. Why is that?

Since I last wrote on NOC about 3-4 years ago, the company has reiterated and strengthened its focus on military hardware for classified programs. The reasons that I am conservative on the company at a high valuation are related to the same reasons as to why I am conservative on the other companies in this sector.

Military contracts are more complex than most investors and analysts seem to take into consideration when investing. They come over a long time, usually lack consistent or comprehensive index clauses, and also share exposure to pension allocations, payment milestones, and other factors that can disguise the true ups and downs for the companies over the short term. That's why these companies sometimes have trends to trade at very low multiples for extended periods of time. That's also why many of the bigger ones, like General Dynamics, try to introduce consumer segments that do not share these characteristics.

Defense budget allocation is something that's almost impossible to forecast - case in point, Russian invasion of Ukraine. It's why I favor the bigger companies with comprehensive, long-term growth profiles above niche companies with one-product or one-segment appeal.

Northrop has a number of projects that enhance its theoretical appeal, such as the Sentinel system (Northrop was the only bidder on this), as well as the B-21 program, awarded about 10 years ago, with a maiden flight in 2023, and entering production not that long ago.

During good times, these companies have the potential to deliver a lot of cash to shareholders because the returns are regulated, the markets are mature, and in the contracts, the government(s) also include funds for R&D, which is rare.

The danger is when the companies in the sector suddenly trade at significant premiums, because these markets don't have that much long-term growth happening. The situation right now is different because we have a different geopolitical situation.

The question then becomes, how long is this going to last?

As a military spending bear (at this particular point of valuation for all of the defense-related companies), I would argue that US military funding is inherently political and uncertain. To base premiums on this is dangerous. To give examples, let's say that SpaceX starts encroaching on space military contracts - this would lower returns and EPS for existing companies, including Northrop. Furthermore, and this is a problem unique to Northrop, much of the company's work is classified. That means that even an analyst like me spends a lot of time fumbling in the dark with regard to future visibility. While not necessarily a problem, we have to discount for uncertainty - and that's why I consider this company's value lower than others do.

Most of the appealing qualities about the company are backward-looking at this time. B-21 bomber programs and space appeal are there, but these contracts are "done" for now, delivering decent growth, but not massive upside.

This sector can, in fact, be categorized as quite an internal contradiction. You have investments of billions of dollars to generate profits on products that represent cutting-edge human military technology. But the profit and appeal decline over time. Because the company in question usually sells to only one or one type of buyer, it's referred to in economic academia as a monopsony. The buyer's needs are rapidly evolving, which means the value of the company's continuing R&D is limited, and the fact that funding is often fought over (politics) makes this not that appealing.

Recent results, 3Q25, were decent, but the current forecast for 2025E EPS growth is 0%. Demand on the top line is strong, and sales increased 5% YoY. Operating margin has increased. Pension sensitivities and forecasts (as I mentioned above) are, however, still a major part of the company's near-term.

It's possible that we'll see continued AEPS growth supported by continued defense spending. The current administration, despite beginning with an inward focus, has taken a focus outward (Venezuela, continued military action in Ukraine) instead. It wouldn't be illogical, based on this, to expect continued sales growth.

At the same time, cost efficiency and cost inflation are two things on everyone's mind. Add to this that Northrop Grumman is currently trading quite significantly above any long-term P/E average. Even if you were to weigh heavily on the shorter-term P/E average, including positive trends, I would say that the company is too expensive for what it offers here.

Therefore, despite encouraging 3Q results and good medium-term trends, I'm not favorably inclined towards NOC at this time. Changes in political priorities could definitely impact the company, and while the parties ordering do offer attractive margins, they do not offer additional payments for cost overruns on huge contracts, which is what Northrop risks.

It's why the 20-year P/E average for this company is actually below 15x P/E - because outside of the current growth environment, fundamentals are not that appealing, and the growth rate is below 4-6% per year. Add to that that NOC doesn't pay a high yield - less than 1.6%—and we have the cherry on top of the current neutral thesis.

Let's look at valuation.

Valuation for Northrop - I don't see a market-beating upside unless you premiumize the company's forward valuation

The issue is therefore not that the company is not attractive. Northrop is a fundamentally attractive business. BBB+ rating, good yield coverage, superb market position, it's all positive.

But what if even after all of that, and even using the highest 5-year average P/E forecast, your returns by investing here would still be negative, even with the yield included?

That is the upside you have to the highest P/E average now. Not great, is it? Even if you were to estimate the company at, say, 22x P/E, the current premium is only 12.85% annualized, less than market-beating. If we use the 20-year company P/E average, the return is even lower.

You would essentially, despite this company being incredibly solid, see significant losses of double digits.

The challenge in establishing an updated valuation lies in going between the conservative and the bullish outlook, and finding a valuation that justifies the company's near- and medium-term potential, while still discounting for risk and company characteristics.

Many analysts consider the military companies undervalued based on current upside and current interest in their products. Some price them at 24-25x P/E, and 16-17x EV/EBITDA with fair value estimates of $600/share or above. I would consider this to disregard the potential for cost impacts, the political risks, and the inherent dangers to the company's ups and downs. I say that there is a reason that over time, these companies have traded below 15x rather than above, and why NOC for many years was close to 10x P/E.

By giving the company a fair value of 15.5x P/E, I believe I am allowing for a great deal of positivity. This implies a fair value of $500/share for the long term, and implies a PT of $450/share.

Based on this, the company is a "HOLD".

Peers would be companies like Boeing (BA) and Lockheed. I consider Boeing to be currently too impacted by its civil aviation troubles, and for an upside here to be too far in the future. Boeing is a "HOLD" to me. Beyond that, LMT is in a better valuation position than NOC is, based also on significantly higher growth rates in 2026E, but still trades somewhat too high for comfort. I therefore consider LMT to be a "HOLD" as well.

I believe investors do better by staying away from most of these investments at this time.

Risks & Considerations

Risks for new technologies and new entrants, as mentioned above, and the inherent risk of being reliant on political and military funding present risks to this company that is relatively unique. In a hypothetical US pullback from the world stage, I would consider many of these companies to be basically uninvestable - because many of them, unlike their European counterparts, are heavily dependent on US military spending.

A word or two also on the aforementioned risk of cost overruns. We have an excellent example for this risk that's very current, and for NOC. The B-21 Raider program has seen several cost overruns, which have resulted in significantly lower profit, and the company has absorbed significant charges in excess of $1.5B due to inflation, supply chain, and manufacturing cost increases. All of these are at the company's risk.

If you premiumize this company or any like it too far, you are saying that the risk of such overruns is low. Recent history would suggest otherwise.

These are the primary risks to the company.

Thesis

At the end, Northrop Grumman is an excellent company that I believe deserves your attention, and it's an excellent company to have in your portfolio, provided you're able to buy it at the right price.

Based on the current valuation and forecast, I don't see an appealing future potential for returns. NOC is too expensively traded, and while growth is forecasted, I believe the current forecasted EPS growth rate incorrectly takes into account the risk for fundamental risks, as well as program risks. Macroeconomic risks come in addition to that.

Fair value for the company is, by my estimate, no more than $500/share, making it a PT of $450/share here, and that makes NOC a "HOLD".

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which is not enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of $450/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "HOLD" rating as of December 2025.

data.title: Permian Basin Shale Gas And Tight Oil And Shale Gas From U.S. Tight Oil Plays
data.description:
A guest post by D C

This post reviews my method for estimating output from tight oil and shale gas plays. The basic method involves convolution, which sounds complicated (or perhaps convoluted) but is fairly simple to accomplish using a spreadsheet. It may be helpful to review my post on this from over 11 years ago at this link.

The two charts above show the average Permian Basin wells by year of first flow from 2010 to 2016 for both tight oil and shale gas for all horizontal wells completed in the Permian Basin. I used the well quality tab in a post by Enno Peters linked here to grab these charts. Links directly to these charts are here for first chart and here for second chart. At each of these links, the data can be explored in greater detail.

The second pair of charts shows average Permian Basin well profiles by year of first flow from 2016 to 2023 for both tight oil and shale gas. Links to the charts are here for tight oil and here for shale gas. Note that after 2020 for tight oil and 2021 for shale gas, the well profiles stop increasing, and in the case of tight oil, decrease after 2022. These charts are not normalized for lateral length. The average lateral length has been increasing from 2010 to 2023. After 2016, the EUR of tight oil for the average Permian tight oil well when normalized to 10 thousand lateral feet has been decreasing since 2016 at roughly 2% per year.

Convolution

We start with an average well profile in our simple model; this is the average 2022 Permian Basin tight oil well profile.

Then we have a chart representing the number of new wells added each month.

Wells are added from month 1 to month 129 in this scenario. The spreadsheet with the scenario can be downloaded here. This represents a very low price scenario - WTI under $45/b in 2025.

For the shale gas output for the scenario above, we use the same chart for new wells added as presented earlier but use the shale gas well profile below.

The spreadsheet is here for this shale gas scenario.

The GOR for this scenario (in the second spreadsheet) is below.

To create a more realistic scenario, we can use three scenarios and stitch them together, as we need about 300 months or more to create a realistic scenario with about 108 thousand completed wells for a low oil price scenario (WTI is $65/b or less). For tight oil, the results of the three spreadsheets are combined in the first here, the second spreadsheet (convolution 2) is here, and the third spreadsheet (convolution 3) is here. For the shale gas scenario, there are also three spreadsheets: one, two, and three.

This is the number of new wells added per month for the scenario. Note that the tight oil and shale gas well profile is unchanged from the short 129-month scenario (very low price scenario). The convolution spreadsheets result in the following output for tight oil and shale gas; see the chart below.

The tight oil scenario has a URR of 47 Gb, and the shale gas scenario has a URR of 219 TCF.

The chart above shows the GOR for the low-price scenario presented in the previous chart. Total wells completed for the scenario is 108 thousand wells. The URR for this scenario is optimistic because no degradation of new well EUR is assumed after 2021. Pressure depletion has been evident since at least 2022 and probably started in 2017 (but is hidden by increasing lateral length). When we account for this by normalizing for lateral length, the degradation in new well EUR begins in 2017.

The two previous charts show how the Average Permian well profile changed from 2012 to 2020. The tight oil well profiles for 2022 and 2023 were very similar to 2020, with 2021 about 4% higher. I assume that the 2024 average new well was similar to 2022 and 2023. For shale gas, the average 2021 well has an EUR about 3.37% higher than 2020, and the 2022 and 2023 wells look very similar to 2021. I assume the 2024 well is also the same EUR as the 2021-2023 average wells.

I assume the average Permian well suffers pressure depletion, which reduces new well EUR, as shown in the two charts above for the low oil and natural gas price scenario presented below. This scenario also assumes about 108 thousand wells are completed from Jan 2010 to August 2040. About 55 thousand horizontal Permian wells have been completed through September 2025. Analyses by Jorge Garzon of Novilabs of the Midland and Delaware sub-basins of the Permian (click links for details) suggest about 52 thousand locations with tier one or tier two economics remain in the Permian. For the low prices that I have assumed (consistent with current futures curves for oil and natural gas), I believe only locations with tier one or tier two economics will be viable.

Spreadsheet with tight oil scenario above can be downloaded here (about 5 megabytes).

Spreadsheet for shale gas output for Permian low-price scenario shown above is here.

Tight oil, shale gas and GOR for the Permian low-price scenario in chart above.

Using EIA data for shale gas and tight oil for the Permian and non-Permian tight oil plays, we find that from 2018 to 2025, the trend in GOR has a similar slope (an increase of about 0.2 MCF/bo per year). The average difference in GOR is about 2 MCF/bo over this period, with non-Permian tight oil having a higher GOR average than the Permian Basin.

To get a rough estimate of US Shale Gas from tight oil plays in a low-price scenario, I use the Permian GOR shown in the chart before the previous chart (second chart up) and add 2 MCF/bo to approximate GOR for non-Permian tight oil.

The chart above has a non-Permian US tight oil scenario for a medium price scenario for oil and natural gas (oil = &75/b and gas = $4/MCF). This is multiplied by the GOR scenario I referenced earlier (2 MCF/bo added to Permian scenario over the Jan 2010 to April 2053 period.)

The scenario above adds the Permian shale gas to the non-Permian tight oil associated shale gas. The model is very approximate, with the GOR used as a shortcut to approximate shale gas output from several distinct tight oil plays (Bakken, Eagle Ford, Niobrara, Woodford, Mississippian, Uinta, and Powder River). Note that over the 12-year period, from Jan 2014 to November 2025, the model mostly overestimates shale gas output, so the model might be on the optimistic side. In addition, the medium-price tight oil scenario probably overestimates non-Permian tight oil for a low-price scenario by 25% or so, which would also tend to make the scenario above too optimistic. In any case, the future is always unknown. Scenario in spreadsheet here.

Above is a model I did in December 2018. My current best estimate for low, medium and high oil price scenario cases has a URR of 42 Gb, 52 Gb and 62 Gb, respectively, roughly between my low and medium cases from 2018. The high case from the 2018 model would probably require an oil price of $250/b in 2025 and natural gas at $10/MCF.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: NANC: Invest In Democrat-Endorsed Securities
data.description:
I don't know about you, but I personally find it HILARIOUS (and disturbing) that apparently all it takes to beat the stock market is to monitor the trades of politicians. Not a day goes by where on X someone screenshots some insane return because they tracked Nancy Pelosi's activity. Is this a legitimate strategy? One that over time works? I don't know, but that's the bet that the Unusual Whales Subversive Democratic Trading ETF (Ticker: NANC) is making. The fund replicates the stock buys and sells that Democratic members of Congress and their spouses have disclosed. Those disclosures come under the STOCK Act, a law obligating lawmakers to report their trades within 45 days. Whatever you think of congressional trading, the filings provide a real-time window into how elected officials invest personal capital. All NANC is doing is systematizing that process by marking its own portfolio to reflect those trades.

I'm honestly laughing while writing this because I can totally understand the investment thesis. Lawmakers are closer than anyone to big money, and impact where money goes. The fund combs through the disclosure reports and constructs its holdings around the names that members of Congress are actually trading. It does not try to predict where lawmakers may head next, and it does not mix in macro views or factor tilts. It's a simple copycat approach, but one that screens out only a single political party (in this case, if you couldn't tell, Democrats).

How Tech Dominance Impacted NANC

Many of the same stocks purchased by Democratic lawmakers over the past few years just so happen to be some of the very companies leading investors’ hopes for an economic acceleration right now. Big Tech simply crushed from 2023 to 2025 and NANC ended up naturally overweight those giants. Significant positions in Nvidia, Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon and Apple followed. That concentration was not the product of a thematic call by the fund’s managers. It was the direct result of congressional filings that kept pointing, over and over again, to Silicon Valley and that broader innovation complex.

Democrats, more than Republicans, love Tech.

The tailwind rarely could have been more favorable. Technology shares leapt on a powerful wave of optimism around artificial intelligence, data-center buildouts and cloud-infrastructure spending. It was Nvidia’s cycle to lose as requests for its AI chips shot up. NANC also benefited as the stock rose, because prominent Democrats remained ahead on accumulating shares long before it became an "obvious" trade.

The timing was downright embarrassingly good. NANC embarked on a two-year rampage during which tech and innovation stocks outperformed the rest of the market, and Democratic lawmakers, whether by luck or belief, also owned those names at scale already. That helped the fund beat traditional benchmarks in its short life. When we look at the price ratio of NANC to SPY, we see it's closer to the top end of the ratio (and also can see most of it's outperformance happened in 2023/early 2024..

What Might Sustain NANC’s Momentum into 2026

The natural question now is whether that luck will extend into 2026. The argument for continued strength is the belief that AI-related spending hasn’t even come close to peaking. Large technology companies are expanding data-center footprints and spending huge sums to build out new compute infrastructure. Decisions like those are hardly ever one-offs. When a company has taken the first steps into AI-powered business models, it’s more likely to increase investment than pull back. For as long as these companies are center stage in the market narrative, a portfolio tilted toward their stocks is likely to profit.

NANC is well poised to do that. Its biggest holdings remain the companies at the heart of the A.I. story. If the theme continues to dominate corporate budgets and investor psychology, the fund may well have another strong year. The strategy has less to do with congressional stock-picking “skill” than on whether and when the lawmakers’ preferred companies keep leading the market.

Risks Investors Cannot Ignore

That said, I don’t want investors to consider the risks insignificant. The fund has high concentration, because significant amount of the assets are committed to a few technology names. Should focus turn to defensives or commodity-catch-up trades, NANC could be stuck in yesterday’s leaders with little capacity for rapid change. Tactical flexibility is not in the design of congressional portfolios. Elected officials often maintain the same names over time, and that 45-day lag for disclosure makes it nearly impossible to be timely.

There are structural dangers, too. The controversy about whether lawmakers should be able to trade stocks has resurged. Some proposals have even suggested doing away with stock ownership of individual stocks by lawmakers entirely. If any of those proposals take off, NANC’s reason for being starts to get muddled. At best, the number of trades to follow would be reduced. At worst, the fund would have no clear strategy to which to adhere.

Election-year dynamics add another layer of uncertainty. Lawmakers’ investment interests can turn on a dime as committee assignments change and political priorities shift. What Democrats may have bought in 2023 does not necessarily translate to what they might find attractive in 2026. The problem NANC faces, however, is that it responds to such changes only after the fact.

That said, the fund’s allure is easy to understand. It also provides a rare, window into the personal investment decisions of Washington’s power brokers. The approach is easy to understand and is oddly in step with one of the most potent forces currently roiling markets. The big question is whether that alignment endures.

data.title: Micron: Exploiting The Overlooked Structural Alpha
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Vega North as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

Micron Technology Inc. (MU) is an attractive "buy," in that it has used the quantum hurdles (the physical limits of sub-60-atom semiconductor manufacturing) that have destroyed other companies' ability to compete to create a high-margin, capital-efficient "competitive moat". In my first iteration I stated Micron had transitioned from being a commodity cyclic producer to a "value capture engine", as Micron's use of memory die integration through chiplets and Compute-In-Memory, allows Micron to recapture the profit dollars that were lost to downstream interposer manufacturers. Additionally, Micron is able to command pricing multiples of 10X for their leading edge products. As Micron has a current "fair value" of $336.56, the company is currently undervalued by 21.8%, as the marketplace has not yet fully factored in the structural changes from volume based production to technology leadership production.

Q1 2026 Results

A very large step up has occurred in Micron's first fiscal quarter 2026 performance. The company reported its highest quarterly revenue of $13.64 billion; this represents an increase from last year of 57%. Gross margin was at 56.8% on a non-GAAP basis, which reflects a structural shift towards high-margin products that are being used in artificial intelligence (AI) and a constrained supply environment. On a non-GAAP basis, Micron's diluted earnings per share (EPS) was $4.78 compared to analysts' estimates. In Q2, Micron projected revenue of $18.7 billion and a gross margin of 68%, which suggests that Micron will be the most effective way for companies to capture value as the world builds out its AI infrastructure.

Q2 2026 Outlook

The outlook for Q1 2026 is optimistic with a strong finish to 2025 in terms of sequential revenue growth, due to the ongoing buildout of AI related infrastructure. Sequential revenue growth will likely slow slightly as the seasonality of the industry is reestablished and hyperscale customers are able to digest recent increases in capacity. HBM demand is expected to be sustained at current levels and gross margin should remain above 40%. The demand being experienced is not a one-time cycle-driven surge, but rather a structural change resulting from the estimated $400 billion hyperscaler investment in the build-out of an AI-based infrastructure that will drive AI accelerator memory-to-logic ratio requirements higher; thus positioning Micron to potentially capture a significant portion of the value creation occurring in the next generation of computing.

Data Center Demand

A structural shift in the data center business has created a higher demand for memory and benefits Micron's long-term prospects. The majority of the AI data center is now an "AI factory," rather than a generalized computing facility; the primary constraint in these facilities is memory bandwidth, not processing speed. As a result, all of Micron's HBM production capacity is currently spoken for through the end of 2026.

Micron also enjoys a significant competitive advantage with respect to the memory technology it supplies to the data center/AI landscape, specifically HBM3E memory technology that supplies 1.2 Terabytes/sec of bandwidth at 30% less power consumption than competing products — an important metric for large-scale customers such as Meta and Google that have very limited ability to expand their data center electrical supply. To further entrench itself in this potentially lucrative market, Micron made the strategic decision to discontinue the 29-year-old consumer branded Crucial line of memory products, effective in late 2025. All of Micron's available wafers will be dedicated to the higher margin data center and AI segments of the memory market. By disciplining its allocation of supply in this manner, Micron can ensure that it remains the fundamental backbone of the AI Era, and captures a larger portion of the estimated $400 Billion global investment in AI related infrastructure.

Growth Drivers

The Production Shift

The transition from two-photon to one-photon production represents the introduction of extreme ultraviolet lithography, which may be the biggest challenge for competition. Within the scope of "two-photon" is the dependence on Deep Ultraviolet (DUV) lithography that utilizes a process called multi-layer patterning or multiple "photon events" to pattern features at a size of 10 nanometers. Within the framework of "one-photon" is the usage of Extreme Ultraviolet (EUV) lithography within Micron's 1-gamma production node; utilizing a single 13.5 nm wavelength photon with the capability of creating a feature in a single exposure or "photon event," Micron is able to reduce the complexity of manufacturing and defects associated with multi-patterning. Micron achieves the perfect blend of capability and cost by utilizing EUV lithography only where it is necessary, while continuing to utilize DUV lithography as an economical option for all other layers that do not require EUV lithography, thus allowing Micron to achieve the highest density of bits and the lowest amount of energy in the industry, while limiting the capital expenditures associated with transitioning to full-EUV lithography by its competitors.

For 13.5-nanometer wavelength light, standard glass will absorb all of the light, therefore, EUV systems are designed using mirrors made up of forty alternating layers of molybdenum and silicon, each layer being 2.8 nanometers thick, allowing for constructive interference and thus reflection of EUV light. The surface quality of the mirror must be almost flawless, with a surface roughness of less than 0.1 nm (less than ten percent of a nanometer), indicating that the deviation allowed is smaller than the diameter of an atom. Therefore, developing and maintaining mirrors that meet these specifications required years of research and development into advanced coating technologies.

Revenue Driver

The sub-60 atom size technology allows Micron to use unique technological properties to produce revenue. At this scale, electrons are confined, and their wave function can be manipulated to produce a programmable resistance state (PRS), which is used to create multi bit per cell (mbpc) architecture, increasing memory density 4-8 times over traditional single bit cell designs, without an associated increase in the cost of the product. The increased memory density allows Micron to charge 3-4 times the revenue per wafer as compared to a traditional single bit cell design, while maintaining similar production costs. The sub-angstrom control over hafnium oxide deposited via atomic layer deposition also provides Micron with the capability to produce gate oxide layers of 1.2-1.5 nm thick, resulting in a 100x reduction in leakage current when compared to silicon dioxide-based materials. This leakage reduction results in 40 percent reductions in power consumption, allowing Micron to command price premiums of 25-30 percent on products targeted to low-power markets such as mobile and edge AI.

The unique deployment of EUV lithography also generates new sources of revenue for Micron due to heterogeneous integration. Since Micron uses 193nm immersion for non-critical features, and 13.5 nm wavelength lithography only for critical metal interconnect layers, Micron achieves the best cost per function. As a result, Micron is able to economically produce chiplet-based architectures where memory dies are co-packaged with logic at 10 micron bump pitch, capturing value that was previously lost to the interposer manufacturer. These advanced packaging methods provide Micron with a revenue stream that is 2.5 times greater than traditional discrete memory sales, while requiring only minor incremental capital investments.

A very significant yet often overlooked contributor to this improved margin is how Micron has transformed its interaction with interposer manufacturers and the rest of the packaging supply chain. As a historical matter, Micron has been a consumer of the products of the interposer specialist companies and the Outsourced Semiconductor Assembly and Test (OSAT) companies, utilizing them to perform the last step in assembling chips. However, since memory is moving towards 2.5D and 3D designs, Micron is now performing the TSV and die stacking technologies internally. Thus, by providing a finished memory "cube", rather than just separate dies, Micron is insourcing the "integration margin" that was previously being earned by packaging intermediary companies. Industry analyst, Trax Technologies, also supports this by saying that Micron's vertical integration into its new Cloud Memory Business Unit has allowed Micron to go from being a component supplier to an integrated solution provider, earning value far beyond component sales alone and possibly cannibalizing the profits of traditional OSATs. This strategic move will protect Micron's yield and allow Micron to charge premiums of up to 10 times what it costs to produce finished HBM3E systems.

In addition to allowing Micron to implement wear-leveling at an atomic scale by engineering the barrier heights to allow for controlled quantum tunneling, the resulting increase in endurance (from 10^5 to 10^7) provides a 100X improvement over existing technologies, enabling new markets such as persistent memory (customers are willing to pay 5-8X the cost of DRAM to store their memory in a storage-class memory application that addresses the latency difference between DRAM and NAND). Additionally, with the ability to modulate the tunneling probability using applied electric fields, Micron has created architectures that enable computation in memory, which performs matrix multiplication directly on memory arrays, thus eliminating data movement bottlenecks for AI inference applications and generating a premium of 10X.

Financial Analysis

Margins

The five-quarter increase in Micron’s margins are a significant indicator of Micron’s ability to improve its operations. The company’s gross margin has increased from ~37% to 47% (FQ4-24 to FQ4-25). This demonstrates that Micron has pricing power during this time of increasing memory demand. Operating margin for Micron has nearly doubled from ~23% to ~36%. This indicates that Micron is effectively managing its operating expenses. Similarly, Micron’s net margin has also increased from ~17% to ~31%. This indicates that Micron’s profitability has greatly increased.

Although, there is some volatility in the trend. Margins did not appear to significantly increase until Q3-25 and Q4-25, when they did accelerate. It appears that the recovery in Micron’s margins are dependent on the cyclical nature of the memory industry rather than solely on operational excellence. There appears to be a large gap between gross and net margins for Micron (~16 points). Therefore, while Micron’s margins have been moving in the right direction, it will be difficult to sustain this trend without additional favorable pricing and/or demand conditions for memory products.

DCF

In addition to the 4.5% terminal growth rate and 10.45% WACC, the DCF model includes several other assumptions that are fundamental to the modeling process. The model expects a median growth rate of 8% in revenue, which is in line with Micron's historical growth rate.

The WACC used within the model is 10.01%, which appears reasonable, but it does not account for all the potential risks associated with the technology transition occurring. For example, if the technology transition is not as smooth as anticipated, and therefore requires increased amounts of R&D expenditures, then the risk premium associated with the WACC should be increased. On the other hand, if Micron is able to create durable technological moats, then the WACC used in the model would be too high.

The model assumes that there will be stability in margins due to Micron's pricing power resulting from supply constraint; this is likely conservative given the oligopolistic nature of the DRAM industry. The model extrapolates the efficiencies of the current hybrid EUV processes and the advantages they provide to the future, however, new paradigms of lithography may evolve and replace them.

More importantly, the model assumes that there will be no technological disruptions and that memory architecture will remain fundamentally the same from now until 2035. However, alternative computing paradigms (e.g., quantum, neuromorphic) could make traditional memory obsolete, thus rendering the terminal value of the company worthless. The 4-5% long-term growth rate range included in the model accounts for this level of uncertainty, with the chosen 4.5% representing an attempt to balance the forces driving the maturity of the DRAM market with those sustaining the premium for technological leadership.

The fair market value for Micron's stock is $336.56, presenting a formidable upside of 21.8% for investors.

Risk

The main threat is that if the AI market will grow at a slower rate (or fail to meet forecasts) as it adopts new technology and as such, the HBM demand will drop significantly, and the price premiums that have been the key driver behind Micron’s rising margins will not exist. If the AI trend is found to be nothing but a "bubble" in which the rapid growth from year to year of HBM disappears, then there will be an abundance of capacity in expensive factories that are producing high-margin products.

Micron mitigates these risks by multiple methods. Although AI training represents the most significant use case of HBM, HBM also supports other uses including inference workloads, high performance computing, advanced graphics, data analytics, and networking, all of which support a need for premium pricing due to their high-bandwidth requirements. This diversity of uses reduces Micron’s reliance on AI as the only source of HBM demand. Even if the AI market does not grow at the forecasted levels, the structural shift toward data intensive computing will continue in many industries. Cloud service providers, researchers using advanced simulation techniques, developers of autonomous vehicles, and others will all still require higher memory bandwidth regardless of whether they develop/adopt AI technologies.

Competitive Landscape

The competitive environment for Micron is defined by an oligopolistic market structure of approximately three major players, namely Micron, Samsung, and SK Hynix. The oligopoly is very rigid and represents greater than 95 percent of total worldwide DRAM production. As a result, there is significant price elasticity on behalf of Micron due to the transition to long-term contracts with hyperscalers, which have removed the volatility associated with commodity spot markets of the past. In addition, Micron has utilized strategic divestitures of low-margin consumers through its exit of the lower-margin consumer segment branded under the name "Crucial", to enable the allocation of excess capacity to higher bandwidth memory (HBM), and other enterprise-focused AI solutions. The combination of both a disciplined supply base, and the elimination of ruinous price competition among the big three will provide Micron with the ability to maintain gross margins that will target fifty percent or better.

Conclusion

Micron’s ability to create products at a quantum scale is far greater than mere technological achievements – they serve as a continuously expanding, irreversible barrier (competitive moat) for all of Micron’s competitors. While competitors will encounter exponentially increasing levels of complexity in their attempt to design and manufacture at atomic scales, Micron is able to convert the quantum nature of physics into revenue-enhancing multipliers through its advancements in density, energy efficiency, and new architectural designs. Micron’s hybrid EUV manufacturing strategy allows the company to achieve maximum capital efficiency, while maintaining the company’s technical leadership position, thereby establishing a self-reinforcing cycle where significantly higher margins are generated from the company’s revenue stream that funds Micron’s future technical advancements. In an industry where being left behind is often fatal, Micron’s mastery of the atomic scale will allow the company to capitalize on a disproportionate amount of value creation as the architecture of memory becomes the backbone of computing related to artificial intelligence.
